thi5:1.0.67: In the twenty-five yearssince I went forthI have not found peace of mind,even for as long as a finger-snap.
thi5:1.0.68: Failing to find peace of heart,corrupted by sensual desire,I cried with flailing armsas I entered a dwelling.
thi5:1.0.69: I approached a nunin whom I had faith.She taught me the Dhamma:the aggregates, sense fields, and elements.
thi5:1.0.70: When I heard her teaching,I retired to a discreet place.I know my past lives;my clairvoyance is purified;
thi5:1.0.71: I comprehend the minds of others;my clairaudience is purified;I've realized the psychic powers,and attained the ending of defilements.I have realized the six kinds of direct knowledge,and fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions.
thi5:1.0.72: Intoxicated by my appearance,my figure, my beauty, my fame,and owing to my youth,I despised other women.
thi5:1.0.73: I adorned this body,so fancy, cooed over by fools,and stood at the brothel door,like a hunter laying a snare.
thi5:1.0.74: I stripped for them,revealing my many hidden treasures.Creating an intricate illusion,I laughed, teasing those men.
thi5:1.0.75: Today, having wandered for alms,my head shaven, wearing the outer robe,I sat at the root of a tree to meditate;I've gained freedom from thought.
thi5:1.0.76: All bonds are cut off,both human and divine.Having wiped out all defilements,I have become cooled and quenched.
thi5:1.0.77: Due to improper attention,I was racked by desire for pleasures of the senses.I was restless in the past,lacking control over my mind.
thi5:1.0.78: Overcome by corruptions,pursuing perceptions of the beautiful,I gained no peace of mind.Under the sway of lustful thoughts,
thi5:1.0.79: thin, pale, and wan,for seven years I wandered,full of pain,finding no happiness by day or night.
thi5:1.0.80: Taking a ropeI entered deep into the forest, thinking:“It’s better that I hang myselfthan I return to a lesser life.”
thi5:1.0.81: I made a strong nooseand tied it to the branch of a tree.Casting it round my neck,my mind was freed.
thi5:1.0.82: “Nandā, see this bag of bones asdiseased, filthy, and rotten.With mind unified and serene,meditate on the ugly aspects of the body:
thi5:1.0.83: as this is, so is that,as that is, so is this.A foul stink wafts from it,it is the fools’ delight.”
thi5:1.0.84: Reflecting in such a way,tireless all day and night,having broken throughwith my own wisdom, I saw.
thi5:1.0.85: Being diligent,properly investigating,I truly saw this bodyboth inside and out.
thi5:1.0.86: Then, growing disillusioned with the body,I became dispassionate within.Diligent, detached,I’m quenched and at peace.
thi5:1.0.87: In the past I worshiped the sacred flame,the moon, the sun, and the gods.Having gone to a river ford,I plunged into the water.
thi5:1.0.88: Undertaking many vows,I shaved half my head.Preparing a bed on the ground,I ate no food at night.
thi5:1.0.89: I loved my ornaments and decorations;and with baths and oil-massages,I pandered to this body,racked by desire for pleasures of the senses.
thi5:1.0.90: But then I gained faith,and went forth to homelessness.Truly seeing the body,desire for sensual pleasure is eradicated.
thi5:1.0.91: All rebirths are cut off,wishes and aspirations too.Detached from all attachments,I've found peace of mind.
thi5:1.0.92: Having gone forth out of faithfrom the lay life to homelessness,I wandered here and there,jealous of possessions and honors.
thi5:1.0.93: Neglecting the highest goal,I pursued the lowest.Under the sway of corruptions,I never knew the goal of the ascetic life.
thi5:1.0.94: I was struck with a sense of urgencyas I was sitting in my hut:“I’m walking the wrong path,under the sway of craving.
thi5:1.0.95: My life is short,trampled by old age and sickness.Before this body breaks apart,there is no time for me to be careless.”
thi5:1.0.96: I examined in line with realitythe rise and fall of the aggregates.I stood up with mind liberated,having fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions.
thi5:1.0.97: While staying at homeI heard the teaching from a monk.I saw the stainless Dhamma,extinguishment, the imperishable state.
thi5:1.0.98: Leaving behind my son and my daughter,my riches and my grain,I had my hair cut off,and went forth to homelessness.
thi5:1.0.99: As a trainee nun,I developed the direct path.I gave up greed and hate,along with associated defilements.
thi5:1.0.100: When I was fully ordained as a nun,I recollected my past lives,and purified my clairvoyance,immaculate and fully developed.
thi5:1.0.101: Conditions are born of causes, crumbling;having seen them as other,I gave up all defilements,I’m cooled and quenched.
thi5:1.0.102: I gave birth to ten sonsin this form, this bag of bones.Then, when feeble and old,I approached a nun.
thi5:1.0.103: She taught me the Dhamma:the aggregates, sense fields, and elements.When I heard her teaching,I shaved off my hair and went forth.
thi5:1.0.104: When I was a trainee nun,my clairvoyance was clarified,and I knew my past lives,the places I used to live.
thi5:1.0.105: I meditate on the signless,my mind unified and serene.I achieved the immediate liberation,extinguished by not grasping.
thi5:1.0.106: The five aggregates are fully understood;they remain, but their root is cut.Curse you, wretched old age!now there’ll be no more future lives.
thi5:1.0.107: My hair mown off, covered in mud,I used to wander wearing just one robe.I saw fault where there was none,and was blind to the actual fault.
thi5:1.0.108: Leaving my day’s meditationon Vulture’s Peak Mountain,I saw the stainless Buddhaat the fore of the mendicant Saṅgha.
thi5:1.0.109: I bent my knee and bowed,and in his presence raised my joined palms.“Come Bhaddā,” he said;that was my ordination.
thi5:1.0.110: “I’ve wandered among the Aṅgans and Magadhans,the Vajjīs, Kāsīs, and Kosalans.I have eaten the almsfood of the nationsfree of debt for fifty years.”
thi5:1.0.111: “O! He has made so much merit!That lay follower is so very wise.He gave a robe to Bhaddā,who is released from all ties.”
thi5:1.0.112: Plowing the fields,sowing seeds in the ground,supporting partners and children,young men acquire wealth.
thi5:1.0.113: I am accomplished in ethics,and I do the Teacher’s bidding,being neither lazy nor restless—why then do I not achieve quenching?
thi5:1.0.114: Having washed my feet,I took note of the water,seeing the foot-washing waterflowing from high ground to low.
thi5:1.0.115: My mind became serene,like a fine thoroughbred steed.Then, taking a lamp,I entered my dwelling,inspected the bed,and sat on my cot.
thi5:1.0.116: Then, grabbing the pin,I drew out the wick.The liberation of my heartwas like the quenching of the lamp.
thi5:1.0.117: “Taking a pestle,young men pound grain.Supporting partners and children,young men acquire wealth.
thi5:1.0.118: Do the Buddha’s bidding,you won’t regret it.Having quickly washed your feet,sit in a discreet place to meditate.Devoted to serenity of heart,do the Buddha’s bidding.”
thi5:1.0.119: After hearing her words,the instructions of Paṭācārā,they washed their feetand retired to a discreet place.Devoted to serenity of heart,they did the Buddha’s bidding.
thi5:1.0.120: In the first watch of the night,they recollected their past lives.In the middle watch of the night,they purified their clairvoyance.In the last watch of the night,they shattered the mass of darkness.
thi5:1.0.121: They rose and paid homage at her feet:“We have done your bidding;we shall abide honoring you,as the thirty gods honor Indra,undefeated in battle.Masters of the three knowledges, we are free of defilements.”
thi5:1.0.122: I used to be in a sorry state.As a childless widow,bereft of friends or relatives,I got neither food nor clothes.
thi5:1.0.123: I took a bowl and a staffand went begging from family to family.For seven years I wandered,burned by heat and cold.
thi5:1.0.124: Then I saw a nunreceiving food and drink.Approaching her, I said:“Send me forth to homelessness.”
thi5:1.0.125: Out of compassion for me,Paṭācārā gave me the going forth.Then, having advised me,she urged me on to the ultimate goal.
thi5:1.0.126: After hearing her words,I did her bidding.The lady’s advice was not in vain:master of the three knowledges, I am free of defilements.
